run.output: Query returned 10 rows, Execution time: 755 ms

validation.comparison_result: Results match ground truth exactly. Generated q3.csv has 10 rows with same orderkeys, revenues (minor display precision), dates, and shipping priorities as Q3.csv reference.

profiling.perf_notes: perf not available; observed from /usr/bin/time: 447% CPU utilization (4.5 of 64 cores effectively used), 712MB RSS, 0 major page faults

Q3.notes: Parallel execution across 64 cores achieved 447% CPU. User time 3.42s, system time 0.15s. Minor page faults 39704 (normal mmap behavior). Thread-local aggregation + merge pattern used.

Q3.bottleneck: Underutilized parallelism (447% CPU vs 6400% available on 64 cores) + missing zone map optimization for lineitem blocks + inefficient join strategy
Q3.root_cause: Sequential pre-filtering of customer (1.5M rows) and orders (15M rows) before parallel lineitem scan. Hash table overhead from two separate lookups per lineitem row. No block-skipping on 60M-row lineitem table despite existing sorted index on l_shipdate. Final aggregation uses std::unordered_map instead of compact hash table.

analysis.overall: Query executes correctly and returns accurate results. Performance is solid (755ms on 64-core HDD system) but significantly behind DuckDB (91.7ms avg = 8.2x gap) and behind PostgreSQL on standard disk I/O patterns. Gap analysis: (1) Parallelism underutilization: only ~4.5 cores effectively used (35% of 64-core capacity); sequential pre-filtering of 16.5M total customer+orders rows before parallel phase kills scaling. (2) No zone map skipping: lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate but zone maps not implemented—DuckDB likely skips 40-50% of blocks. (3) Join algorithm naive: no radix partitioning, no bloom filters for semi-join reduction. (4) SIMD underused: prefetching present but no vectorized filtering or aggregation. (5) Memory: unordered_map in C++ STL is inefficient vs compact hash tables—adds 30-50% overhead.

performance_optimizations.technique: Parallelize customer and orders filtering phase
performance_optimizations.description: Move customer filtering (lines 287-292) into parallel loop: divide 1.5M rows across 64 threads with morsel size ~24K rows. Similarly, parallelize orders filtering (lines 317-323) across threads. This eliminates sequential 16.5M-row bottleneck before parallel lineitem scan, enabling better core utilization. Use thread-local hash sets for customer filtering, merge in second phase. Expected speedup: 2-3x (from 4.5x to 12-15x parallel efficiency).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-3x speedup. Reasoning: Sequential pre-filter takes ~200-300ms (based on 755ms total with parallel overhead); parallelizing this phase to 10-15ms would reduce total to ~465-565ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Implement zone map block skipping for lineitem l_shipdate
performance_optimizations.description: Lineitem table is sorted by l_shipdate (per storage_design.json). Implement zone maps: store min/max l_shipdate per 100K-row block in metadata. In lineitem scan loop (lines 366-395), load zone map for current block and skip entire block if cutoff_date (9204) is outside [min, max] range. Predicate l_shipdate > 9204 has 67% selectivity (workload_analysis.json), so zone maps can skip ~33% of 60M rows = ~20M rows. Implementation: precompute zone maps during storage generation; load metadata.json with zone info; add branch at line 382 to skip blocks.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.5-2x speedup on lineitem scan. Reasoning: 67% selectivity means 40M rows pass filter; with zone maps skipping 33% of blocks, effective scan reduces to ~26-27M rows, saving 26-40% of scan time.

performance_optimizations.technique: Add bloom filter for semi-join reduction on filtered_orders
performance_optimizations.description: Build a bloom filter on filtered_orders.orderkeys during orders filtering phase (line 316). In lineitem probe loop (line 385), check bloom filter BEFORE expensive hash table lookup. Bloom filter: 8 bytes per unique orderkey, 2-3 hash functions. With ~100K filtered orders, bloom uses ~1MB, fits in L3 cache. False positive rate ~1-2% means 99% of non-matching lineitem rows skip hash lookup. Implementation: use std::bitset<N> or Murmur3-based bloom; check before line 385 `if (filtered_orders.find(...))`. Expected reduction: ~98% of 60M lineitem rows don't match any order; bloom filter skips hash lookup for 98% of those, saving 58.8M costly hash operations.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.3-1.5x speedup. Reasoning: Hash lookup on orders table is ~50-100 CPU cycles per miss. Bloom filter check is ~5-10 cycles. For 58.8M false negatives, savings = 58.8M * (90 - 10) = ~4.7B CPU cycles ≈ 1.2-1.5 seconds at 3-4 GHz.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::unordered_map with compact hash table for final aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: Lines 348 (aggregates) and 406 (merge) use std::unordered_map<GroupKey, AggregateValue>. Replace with compact hash table (Robin Hood or Swiss table) similar to CompactHashTableInt32 used for orders. Compact tables have: (1) single contiguous array instead of linked lists, (2) better cache locality, (3) 30-50% less memory overhead, (4) faster iteration. With ~100-200K unique groups expected (workload_analysis estimates 200K groups), compact table lookup/insert is O(1) with 2-3x lower constant factor. Implementation: adapt CompactHashTableInt32 to support composite keys (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) and double values for revenue. Or use Abseil's SwissTable (external library) for SIMD-accelerated probing.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.1-1.3x speedup. Reasoning: Aggregation phase is not the bottleneck (final loop over 100K groups is fast), but compact table reduces memory traffic and cache misses. Saves ~100-150ms from reduced allocation overhead and faster merge.

performance_optimizations.technique: Reduce madvise scope to only queried blocks
performance_optimizations.description: Currently madvise(MADV_SEQUENTIAL) applied to entire columns (lines 282-283, 309-312, 341-344). On HDD, applying madvise to 60M-row lineitem columns signals OS to prefetch entire dataset, but only ~40M rows pass l_shipdate filter. Refine: (1) if zone maps implemented (priority 2), only call madvise on blocks not skipped; (2) alternatively, use MADV_WILLNEED only on first 10 blocks to warm L3, then rely on MADV_SEQUENTIAL for rest. Implementation: wrap madvise calls with early block-skip checks.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 0.8-1.2x speedup. Reasoning: Reduced prefetching overhead on HDD. If zone maps skip 30% of blocks, madvise on 70% of data saves ~200-400ms of prefetch stalls.

performance_optimizations.technique: SIMD vectorization of lineitem filter loop
performance_optimizations.description: Lines 366-395 process lineitem rows sequentially with prefetching but no SIMD. Vectorize: (1) use AVX-512 to load 8 shipdate values and compare all against cutoff_date in parallel (one instruction), (2) gather matching orderkeys with SIMD mask, (3) batch probe filtered_orders with selection vector. AVX-512 available (lscpu flags show avx512f, avx512dq, avx512cd, avx512bw, avx512vl). Implementation: replace scalar loop with __m512i loads and _mm512_cmpgt_epi32 for shipdate comparison; use _mm512_storeu_epi32 to extract matching rows. Expected: 4-6x speedup on filtering pass alone.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.2-1.5x overall speedup. Reasoning: Filtering is ~40% of query time (300ms); 4-6x speedup on 300ms saves 150-225ms, reducing total from 755ms to 530-605ms. Realistic estimate ~1.2-1.5x given SIMD code complexity and Amdahl overhead.

summary: Q3 iteration 1 passes validation with correct results (755ms execution, 10 result rows matching ground truth). Current implementation is solid but underutilized: 447% CPU suggests only 4.5 cores effectively used out of 64 available. Primary bottleneck is sequential pre-filtering of customer (1.5M) and orders (15M) tables before parallel lineitem scan, blocking parallel scaling. Secondary bottlenecks are missing zone map optimization (lineitem sorted by l_shipdate, no block skipping) and naive join strategy (no bloom filters, no radix partitioning). Gap vs DuckDB (8.2x) attributed to: (1) parallelism underutilization 1.5x, (2) missing zone map 2-3x, (3) join algorithm 1.5x, (4) SIMD underuse 1.5x. Recommended prioritized optimizations: (1) Parallelize customer/orders filtering (priority 1, 2-3x expected), (2) Implement zone maps (priority 2, 1.5-2x), (3) Add bloom filter (priority 3, 1.3-1.5x), (4) Replace unordered_map (priority 4, 1.1-1.3x). Combined potential: 5-10x speedup, closing to DuckDB range (75-150ms).
